text.title: Validators, Filters, and Hallucination Detection
text.summary: OutputGuardrails validator/filter composition pattern, common validators (profanity, JSON format, length), and the HallucinationDetector LLM-based check.

rule.statement: Separate validators (boolean: is this output acceptable?) from filters (transform: make this output acceptable). Run validators first; only apply filters if the output is not blocked.
rule.rationale: Mixing validation and filtering logic makes it impossible to audit why an output was blocked vs. modified.

rule.statement: Require a context document before enabling hallucination detection; set enable_hallucination_detection=False by default — enable only for high-stakes outputs where a context is always available.
rule.rationale: Without ground truth, LLM-based hallucination detection is guesswork that adds cost and latency with no reliability benefit.

rule.statement: Do not run hallucination detection synchronously in the request path for every call — queue it as async post-processing and surface results in a review UI.
rule.rationale: Hallucination detection doubles cost and adds 200–800ms; running it on every call makes it a reliability bottleneck.

rule.statement: Use the guardrails-ai library for standard validators (ToxicLanguage, ValidLength, RegexMatch, ValidJSON) rather than writing them from scratch.
rule.rationale: 50+ battle-tested validators exist; re-implementing them introduces gaps in edge-case coverage.

example.code:
from guardrails import Guard from guardrails.validators import ValidLength, ToxicLanguage
guard = Guard().use_many( ValidLength(min=1, max=1000, on_fail="fix"), ToxicLanguage(threshold=0.5, on_fail="filter"), ) result = guard( llm_api=openai.chat.completions.create, prompt="Tell me about AI safety", model="gpt-4o", max_tokens=500 ) if result.validation_passed: print(result.validated_output)
